MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion For Ccp 128.5 Fee-Shifting Sanctions Re: Premier Estates And Anton Danilovich'S Memorandum Of Costs; Memorandum Of Points & Authorities In Support
Off Calendar Nov-22-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MARK POE, Esq. of Gaw/Poe (Tel: 415-766-7451) for plaintiff;
Atty. CHRISTOPHER KARIC, Esq. of Sellar, Hazard & Lucia (Tel: 650-248-1724) for defendant Premier Estates.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF 29 SW, LLC's Motion For CCP 128.5 Fee-Shifting Sanctions Re: Premier Estates And Anton Danilovich's Memorandum Of Costs.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for sanctions is granted in the amount of $11,676.80, to be paid jointly and severally by Defendants and their counsel. The Court declines to consider Defendants' late-filed opposition to the motion. (Cal. R. Ct. 3.1300(d).) As Plaintiff points out, this is the third time that Defendants have missed a filing deadline, without any attempt to seek leave of Court or to demonstrate good cause for having failed to file timely.

For the reasons explained at length in the Court's November 8, 2021 order, Defendants' opposition to Plaintiff's motion to strike was frivolous. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 128.5(b)(2) ["totally and complete without merit"]; In re Marriage of Sahafzadeh-Taeb & Taeb (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 124, 135 ["Whether an action is frivolous is governed by an objective standard: any reasonable attorney would agree it is totally and completely without merit."].) The Court further finds that Defendants filed their memorandum of costs and opposed Plaintiff's motion to strike in subjective bad faith. (See In re Marriage of Sahafzadeh-Taeb, 39 Cal.App.5th at 141 ["with an improper motive, such as to harass or manipulate opposing counsel or the court"].) In their memorandum of costs, Defendants sought over $300,000 in attorneys' fees to which they were not entitled because they are not prevailing parties within the meaning of Civil Code section 1717. In their opposition to Plaintiff's motion to strike, they failed even to identify a contractual or statutory basis for that fees claim, and indeed insisted at the hearing that no such basis is required. Further, by Defendants' own admission, the vast bulk of the fees they sought were incurred in other actions, one of which they lost more than four years ago, and a second of which is pending in another county and is not set for trial until January 2022. "[W]hen a tactic or action utterly lacks merit, a court to entitled to infer the party knew it lacked merit yet pursued the action for some ulterior motive." (In re Marriage of Sahafzadeh-Taeb, 39 Cal.App.5th at 144 (citation and quotations omitted).) The Court finds that Defendants' actions were taken in bad faith, that is, without subjective good faith or honest belief in the propriety or reasonableness of their actions, and with the evident intention of harassing or retaliating against Plaintiff and its counsel for having filed this litigation against them. Under the circumstances, an award of sanctions is warranted. (See id. at 141 ["In short, a lawyer whose dereliction in duty is 'blatant' and who has 'personally misused the judicial system' can be required to pay associated costs needlessly incurred by an opposing party"].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

Off Calendar Jun-25-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS ANTON DANILOVICH and PREMIER ESTATES, INC.'S Amended Motion for Leave To File Cross Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Premier Estates, Inc.'s motion for leave to file a cross-complaint is granted. Plaintiff concedes "it is true that both sets of claims concern the same residential property." "A motion to file a cross-complaint at any time during the course of the litigation must be granted unless bad faith of the moving party is demonstrated where forfeiture would otherwise result." (Silver Organizations Ltd. v. Frank (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 94, 99.) Moreover, permissive cross-claims are liberally allowed in California courts. (See CCP 428.10.)

Order signed.
